All right, you're back. Time to roll forward. This is an awesome lesson. We focus on three different things from one question. Firstly, we go through what do you do if you're actually struggling with creating a vision statement? I see this all over the world. People have great intentions. They want to create this bold, bright vision for their future. But when it comes time to actually execute, you can't do it. Either it's a limiting belief that's holding them back or something else. So in this lesson, Victoria extracts at me exactly what all my hacks are. I've used all over the world, various people of all different industries and of all different levels of intelligence and education so anyone can create a compelling vision for their future. In addition to that, then it also then morphs into the power of your why. And I really dig into my own why back when I was in jail, which is a powerful story. You probably haven't heard it before, right? But then also how do you use your own why to write your vision statement and uncover your why at the deepest level. And then thirdly, what is my personal secret of how I harness the power of my own fears and insecurities and actually use those as a propulsion system to ensure my own success. See so many people out there, they let their insecurities and their fears disempower them and we all have those fears and insecurities. I'd be lying to you if I say it didn't happen. I do. We all have them. But it's our ability to overcome them to move into those fears, which is what courage is all about by the way. That's what courage is, is essentially act in the face of fear. But how do you do that? How do you develop that courage, that muscle that allows you to essentially step into your fears and turn them into assets, propulsion systems for your own success? Check this out. It's awesome. Sit back and enjoy the ride. Now we'll see you on the other side. To develop something so detailed, obviously entails quite a bit of brain power, a bit of creative thinking. I know you have that ability. What advice would you give to someone who's struggling trying to create that vision, just doesn't know where to start, doesn't know where to see themselves? I don't think it takes much brain power past maybe a seventh or eighth grade education. I don't think it's brain power. It's not that. It's commitment to actually sitting down and doing it. If you can write English or whatever language you speak, and you are smart enough to have gotten a job and you're, I'm doing this into you for this training program, if you're sitting watching this right now, you're smart enough to do a vision statement. So that's not what's holding you back. If you're being held back right now from writing your vision statement, you're simply just not trying. You have the skill sets to do it. So what I would do in that case, let's say you're blocked. You just can't, for some reason you can't get off the mark. You can't get past square one. I would start in a different way. I'd say rather than my vision, let's start off with my why first. Why is success important to me? Why do I really want to achieve? And just to say, let's call your vision achievement. Why do I want to have achievement in my life? Why does it matter? Who, what are the things in my life that really are important that are really my drivers, right? At the deepest level, we always move away from things that cause us pain and we gravitate towards things that give us pleasure. So I would say what in your life gives you ultimate pleasure? What does ultimate pleasure mean to you? And what does ultimate pain mean to you? It's very different for me than it is to you. Everyone's different. We all have our own beliefs, our own experiences, our own values. And based on those beliefs, values and experiences, we develop ideas of what means ultimate pleasure to us. And can you share your personal why, Jordan, with us? Yeah, of course. Especially I'll share my most compelling why I've ever had in my life. And that was when I was in jail, had lost everything. Okay, my wife had left me, my children had moved to a different state. I'd been smeared and vilified in the press. I had lost my money. I lost my freedom. I was at ground zero in jail. And by sheer happenstance, I happened to be in the same cube as Tommy Chong from Cheech and Chong. Go figure. They put me the 10 year sober back then guy in the cube of the world's biggest drug addict, Tommy Chong. Pot addict, at least. He was a great guy, a very famous comedian if you don't know who he is, Tommy Chong. And we started telling each other stories and it was Tommy who said to me, geez, you have the craziest life ever. You got to write a book about it. So Tommy was the one that really first inspired me to start writing a book. To make a long story short, when I actually emerged from prison, I had written about it to be 80 to 100 pages. I ripped them up because I wasn't happy with those pages. And I started again. And when I got to page 10, page 10 or 12, I was able to get those pages to an agent. The agent fell in love with those pages and said, you've got something there that's amazing and so on and so forth. You got to become a writer. You have no idea what your life is in for. And he said, all these wonderful things to me, but deep down, I was nervous. I didn't really have that much self-belief in my ability to write a book. I had never written before. So I said, all right, I'm going to do this. I want to do this. I wrote my vision statement. I'm going to be a world. I was back then it was to be a world-class author, a bestseller all over the world, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, right? And then I did something that was probably the most powerful thing I ever did in my life. I said to myself, who should I tell? In who in my life should I tell? Who should I describe my vision to? And I went to my back then to my 12-year-old daughter, who's a real daddy's girl. And I just had so much guilt over having to leave her those years. And I said to my daughter, I said, guess what? Daddy's going to become a bestselling author. I'm going to write this book. And by the way, I did not think I could ever do it. I was so much self-doubt about writing, but I knew this. If I told my daughter that I was going to become an author, I'd give myself no choice but to succeed because to be a failure in the eyes of my daughter meant ultimate pain to me. I would do anything to avoid that pain of letting my daughter down again and to be a success in my daughter's eyes. And of course, my son's eyes as well, he's a bit younger back then, to be a success in my children's eyes, to me was ultimate pleasure, more viable than money. It's really right up there with having my health, to be a success and to be looked up to in the eyes of my children. In my darkest days in prison, when I was teaching myself to write, it was the faces of my children. That was my why. That was what kept me going. And when I went to write this book, and it was took 10, 11 months of my life, 16, 17 hours a day to write The Wolf of Wall Street being a nonwriter. I wasn't a writer. I had to teach myself to write as I was writing. It was the my why was to become a success in the eyes of my children. Now, some people would say, oh, well, that's not really healthy. The base, how about this? I don't care. I knew what I, to me, I knew what I needed. I knew that I know the power of understanding your why. I said, if I know why, I really want to be successful. I'll take that and work it in my favor. And I use that to motivate as an incredibly powerful, self-motivating force, the love of my children. And I almost tricked myself, played this mental game that if I failed my children, they'd still love me. I know that. But I use that to empower myself. We spend as human beings probably 75% of our day almost working against ourselves. We're like, we're self-defeating on thoughts, right? Well, I say, why not make your thoughts, you know, moving you towards success instead of being self-defeating, make them self-winning your thoughts, right? So I use my own psychology as a propulsion system towards success, figuring out exactly why I need to succeed and hooking that into my day-to-day activity. I never had a problem motivating myself to write the book. And I wrote it and it became a bestseller and everything happened from there. You get it? My daughter, my son, I mean, I knew that while maybe I wouldn't perform for myself, my own grandeur in the future, right? To be famous again. Yeah, that's great, you know, what you'll do for yourself. But the thought of letting my little girl down, again, impossible, not going to happen, to not be a role model for my son, no way, under no circumstances. I will run for a wall of fire. That's what our real why is about. So please take the time now to really make sure that this is truly your deepest why. Whatever you wrote down in the last quiz, last exercise, ask yourself, is it true? Am I being completely authentic or is there something else? Remember, it's never going to be about something you want or you don't need to have. Even if it's a deep need, our why, the ones that really move us to take action are about the people that we love unconditionally and the causes that we truly care about. That's what a great why does. So let's go to the quiz section now, make sure you go through this every last detail. And when you're done, meet me in the next lesson.
